---
title: "Labor OS and Typed Jobs"
chapter_id: "labor-os-and-typed-jobs"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "talos"
  - "viea"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "talos_md"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact source-note mappings for the core claim; support remains at argument until scheduler, permission, adapter, or replay harnesses exist."
  - "Protocol schema/fixture validation and narrow Lean lifecycle/approval predicates are implemented; no job scheduler, permission-enforcement harness, adapter runner, or replay harness has been run for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Labor OS and Typed Jobs

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `labor-os-and-typed-jobs` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `talos`, `viea`, `genesiscode`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `talos_md`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `talos`; supporting: `viea`, `genesiscode`, `software_magic_grimoire`; variant: `talos_md`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `talos`, `viea`, `genesiscode`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `talos_md`, `moecot`; raw cache: `talos`, `viea`, `genesiscode`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `talos_md`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | Typed job fixture validation implemented; finite lifecycle and approval predicates implemented in `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs`; scheduler, permission, adapter, and replay tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats typed jobs as the execution contract for work, not as proof that a labor OS is implemented. The repo validates a typed-job fixture and now proves two finite Lean predicates over lifecycle and approval records, but it has not run a job scheduler, permission harness, adapter runner, approval service, or replay system for this chapter.

## Problem

Intelligence must become typed work with lifecycle, permissions, artifacts, logs, and approvals.

The previous chapters decide what should be believed and reviewed. Execution begins when accepted plans become work. Talos supplies the right frame: an AI labor operating system turns plans into typed jobs with contract locks, permissions, isolated workspaces, adjudication, delivery, feedback, audit, and replay.

A job is the unit that keeps agency bounded. It carries a contract, lifecycle state, inputs, outputs, permissions, approval state, runtime adapter, failure behavior, audit events, and replay status.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Chat outputs do not provide deterministic job lifecycle, tool isolation, auditability, or repeatable cognitive manufacturing.

Chat is not a factory. It does not lock a contract, isolate tools, enforce permission boundaries, require approvals, preserve logs, or tell a later verifier how to replay what happened. Without jobs, the system has vibes and side effects instead of controlled work.

GenesisCode reinforces the distinction: proposals and effects are different things. Effects require capability boundaries and replay logs. Software Magic adds that workflows need entry rules, exit rules, guards, loops, and recovery paths.

## Core Claim

[labor-os-and-typed-jobs.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] The execution layer should convert plans into typed jobs managed by a governed labor operating system.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Talos supplies the labor-OS and job-lifecycle vocabulary; VIEA supplies the intent-to-artifact trace; GenesisCode supplies effect-boundary and replay-log discipline; Software Magic Grimoire supplies workflow guard vocabulary; the Markdown Talos release is a terminology variant, not independent benchmark evidence.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this labor-OS claim to all assigned source notes. The mappings support typed jobs, lifecycle gates, permissions, evidence records, replay, and approval boundaries as execution architecture, not a working scheduler or runtime.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `talos` | Typed job lifecycle, decomposition, contract lock, factory execution, adjudication, delivery, feedback/autopoiesis, deterministic control planes, audit, replay, evidence, isolation, and approval-gate failure modes. | No Talos scheduler, approval service, runtime, replay log, benchmark, or security result has been reproduced here. |
| `viea` | Intent lowering into command contracts, artifacts, specialist-routed work, verified outputs, runtime execution, feedback, residuals, tools, benchmarks, and regression coverage. | No deployed intent-to-job pipeline or verified execution trace exists here. |
| `genesiscode` | Proposal/effect separation through capability boundaries, deterministic effect logs, provenance, obligations, semantic patches, and small trusted-core discipline. | No local GenesisCode implementation, effect runner, replay checker, proof artifact, or security audit exists here. |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Bounded instructions with roles, objectives, context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, failure behavior, guards, entry/exit rules, loops, recursion, and recovery paths. | Workflow vocabulary only; not empirical evidence that a labor OS performs better or safely executes work. |
| `talos_md` | Public-release Talos terminology around typed AI labor, deterministic manufacturing, five-phase lifecycle, control-plane ledgers, evidence, isolation, auditability, blind handles, and Digital SCIFs. | Variant/canonicalization aid only; reported benchmark or security claims remain unverified. |
| `moecot` | Runtime-reference context for governed orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, promotion blockers, and residual tracking. | Runtime code, logs, benchmark artifacts, and replay records have not been inspected or reproduced here. |

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Typed Job lifecycle.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Plan node"] --> B["Typed job"]
  B --> C["Contract lock"]
  C --> D["Permission check"]
  D --> E{"Approval required?"}
  E -- "yes" --> F["Approval gate"]
  E -- "no" --> G["Dispatch"]
  F --> G
  G --> H["Runtime adapter"]
  H --> I["Artifact + audit events"]
  I --> J["Adjudication / delivery"]
  J --> K["Feedback + replay status"]
```

- Represent jobs with type, inputs, tools, permissions, expected artifacts, approval gates, and failure behavior.
- Separate orchestration, secure workspace/logistics, security/access controls, secret isolation, and audit as explicit execution subfunctions.
- Isolate tools and runtime adapters.
- Record job lifecycle and evidence artifacts.

The lifecycle should be explicit enough to reject invalid transitions. A job can be drafted, locked, awaiting approval, dispatchable, running, adjudicating, delivered, failed, blocked, replayed, or retired. High-impact work should stop at the approval gate until the required record exists.

The execution layer should not merge all subfunctions into one agent. Orchestration, secure workspace setup, access control, adapter invocation, artifact capture, adjudication, delivery, feedback, and replay are different boundaries.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Typed Job.

- Planning dispatches jobs.
- Security mediates permissions.
- Artifact graph receives outputs.

Minimum fields:

- `job_id`
- `contract_id`
- `job_type`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `runtime_adapter`
- `inputs`
- `outputs`
- `permissions`
- `approval_state`
- `failure_behavior`
- `audit_events`
- `replay_status`

Planning dispatches jobs. Security mediates permissions. Runtime adapters perform effects. Artifact graphs receive outputs, logs, and replay metadata.

## Invariants

- Every job has type and lifecycle state.
- Tool permissions are explicit.
- Human approval gates block high-impact jobs.

The approval invariant is non-negotiable: a job requiring approval cannot execute because a planner or model predicts approval would be granted. The approval record is part of the job state.

## Failure modes

- Tool overreach.
- Unlogged side effects.
- Infinite or chaotic agent swarms.

Tool overreach is a permission failure. Unlogged side effects are audit failures. Chaotic agent swarms are lifecycle failures: work escapes the job boundary and becomes unreviewable. Each should block dispatch or force quarantine.

## Minimal implementation

A typed-job schema with lifecycle states and permission checks.

The smallest useful implementation is the expanded `typed_job` schema and fixture. It validates lifecycle, approval, failure behavior, permissions, audit events, and replay status without claiming a real job was executed.

The fixture validates the job record surface. `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs` adds a bounded transition predicate for declared lifecycle moves and a bounded approval predicate showing that an approval-required job cannot be allowed to run without an approval record. This does not prove an adapter permission check, scheduler implementation, approval-service integration, or replay result.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Typed job fixture validation | Check that the typed-job fixture matches the public schema and declares contract, type, lifecycle state, adapter, inputs, outputs, permissions, approval state, failure behavior, audit events, and replay status. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Typed job lifecycle predicate | Check that a transition record marked valid uses the declared finite lifecycle relation. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs`; checked by Lean build |
| Tool permission enforcement test | Check that declared permissions cover requested adapter capabilities. | planned; not run |
| Human approval gate predicate | Check that an approval-required job cannot be allowed to run before approval is recorded. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs`; checked by Lean build |

Fixture-shape validation and two bounded Lean predicates are implemented. The remaining tests require a lifecycle checker, permission harness, approval service, adapter runner, or job runner that can reject invalid transitions, missing approvals, and unauthorized effects in an executable environment.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:jobs.lifecycle.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs` | A job transitions only through valid lifecycle states. | implemented |
| `lean:jobs.lifecycle.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs` | A job requiring approval cannot execute before approval is recorded. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates. They prove the bounded lifecycle and approval claims stated above; they do not prove the existence or correctness of a full labor-OS runtime.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `talos_md` | Talos_Protocol_v1.0.md | labor_execution_os | Markdown/public release version. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk identifies sources for labor-OS execution discipline. It does not claim a Talos or MoECOT labor runtime has been executed in this repo.

## Summary

Labor OS and Typed Jobs turns plans into controlled work. It gives each executable unit a type, contract, lifecycle, permissions, approval state, adapter, artifacts, failure behavior, audit events, and replay status.

The result is not less autonomy. It is autonomy that can be inspected, approved, replayed, repaired, and improved.
